entry.title: Re: Use which business contexts for the Custom Fields in...
entry.content:
To help you add Custom Fields for delivery documents, you can kindly use the following business contexts:

  • Shipping: Delivery
  • Shipping: Delivery Item

(Please Note, the above business contexts apply to all delivery documents (including outbound delivery and inbound delivery). SAP currently does not support business contexts specifically for inbound delivery only.)

You can kindly refer to the SAP standard documentation: App Extensibility for Delivery Documents | SAP Help Portal to know more details about Custom Fields for delivery documents.

entry.title: Re: Enhancements to Event-Based Variance Calculation with OP2025
entry.content:
The reintroduction of mixed price variances and scrap variances brings event-based processing meaningfully closer to functional parity with the classic approach, and the extension of ACDOCP to capture procurement alternative-level detail is especially important. That part of your post stood out to me.

I'd like to share an observation from a live implementation in very large-scale discrete electronics manufacturing.

In this environment, the main challenge appears one layer upstream of variance categorization itself. We see orders whose actual costs depend on outputs from other orders that are also still being settled within the same period, creating cross-order cost dependencies that cannot be cleanly resolved in sequence. Shared intermediates are consumed across multiple concurrent orders, and the actual cost state of a given cost object is still evolving at the point when variance logic is already being applied to orders that depend on it.

In that setting, the variance split can be internally consistent within the current model, yet still be assigned to cost objects whose own actual cost is not yet settled. The calculation may be formally correct within SAP's framework, but those inter-order dependencies push beyond the boundary conditions under which object-level results remain economically stable.

We have not seen this resolved through configuration or settlement design choices alone, so it seems closer to an architectural boundary condition than to a project-specific setup issue.

Has this class of inter-order dependency come up in your customer conversations as a scenario where the current cost object model reaches its limits — and if so, how has SAP typically framed it?

Perform the below actions to find out the root cause

entry.title: Re: Error When Reverse GI. Stock Stuck in Blocked Stock I...
entry.content:
This is not a bug but a configuration issue. Movement type 687 posts stock into blocked transit, often used in returns, quality issues, or special scenarios. If this was not intended, it suggests either:

  • Wrong delivery type used (e.g., returns delivery instead of standard outbound).
  • Wrong item category determination.

Check what item category is there in delivery. Then go to 0184 and see against which delivery type, this item category is used. Next go to vov7, select that item category and see whether any special stock indicator is set. If item category is correct but the movement type is wrong, then, check the corresponding sales order which schedule line category is flowing. Go to vov5, check whether the right combination of item category to schedule line is configured. These are the root causes for your issue.

entry.title: 1H 2026 Release Alert: New Granular Permissions for TIH & GP Import/Export using Integration Center
entry.content:
As part of our ongoing commitment to data security and privacy, the 1H 2026 Release introduces a more granular Role-Based Permission (RBP) framework for the Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH) and Growth Portfolio (GP).

If your organization uses the Integration Center to automate the import or export of talent data, this update requires your immediate attention to prevent scheduled job failures.

What is Changing?

Previously, integration permissions for TIH were broader. Starting with the 1H 2026 release, we are introducing specific permissions that connects administrative actions with object-level access. This ensures that only authorized administrators can move sensitive talent data in and out of the system.

Action Required: Update Your RBP Settings

To ensure your integrations continue to run without interruption, please audit and update your administrator roles in your Preview environment according to the requirements below.

1. The Mandatory Permissions

Regardless of whether you are importing or exporting, the following administrative settings are now required:

  • [NEW] Administrator Permissions —> Manage Talent Intelligence Hub → Import/Export Talent Intelligence Hub and Growth Portfolio Data

  • Administrator Permissions —> Manage Talent Intelligence Hub → View Manage Talent Intelligence Hub Settings

  • Administrator Permissions —> Manage Integration Tools → Integration Center

2. Object-Level Access (The Fine Print)

In addition to the administrative toggles above, the system now checks for specific object permissions to ensure the user has the right to see or modify the underlying data.

TaskRequired User Permission (Object-Level)Applicable Objects
Exporting DataView PermissionGrowth Portfolio, Attributes, and other TIH Objects
Importing DataCreate PermissionGrowth Portfolio, Attributes, and other TIH Objects

Recommended Next Steps

  1. Review Integration Center Logs: Identify all jobs currently targeting TIH or Growth Portfolio entities.

  2. Audit Service Users: Check the RBP roles assigned to the "Execution User" of these integrations.

  3. Test in Preview: Apply the new Import/Export Talent Intelligence Hub and Growth Portfolio Data permission in your Preview instance immediately following the 1H 2026 release.

  4. Verify: Run a "Test" execution in Integration Center to confirm that the "Permission Denied" errors are resolved.

  5. Prepare for May: During the 1H 2026 Production Release, ensure your instance is updated on Day 1 to avoid any service interruptions for your scheduled jobs.

Keeping your data secure is a collaborative effort, and these more granular controls provide the precision needed for modern data governance.

Have questions about these RBP changes? Leave a comment below or join our upcoming Release Q&A webinar!

entry.title: Week 4 : Use Joule Predictive Code Completion to Implement Determination
entry.content:
Congratulations to everyone who came along with us till the 4th week of the ABAP Developer Challenge. So far we have seen :

  • How to create a OData UI Service from Scratch using Joule Chat
  • How to use Joule - Explain feature to understand a block of code.
  • How to use Joule - RAP Predict Business Logic to create logic for specific tasks.

Let us wrap up with another interesting feature of Joule's ABAP AI Capability - The Joule Predictive Code Completion.

Quick Links

Please use this separate thread to ask your questions and discuss issues. 

Sample Scenario

We had asked you to create a field of type 'Date' during the RAP application creation.

Now let us use Determination function to calculate the number of years since the value in Date field. You can implement it while creating a new record using the app.

Task1: Create a determination and implement the corresponding method.

  1. Define a determination in the BDEF of the CDS view.Define Determination
  2. Now you can notice a small icon to the left of the determination definition. Click on it to create the implementation method for adding determination logic in the local handler class of the behavior pool of your entity. Save and activate the changes.Implement DeterminationFor additional information on Determinations and its syntax and use you can refer the BDEF ‘/DMO/R_Travel_D’. Alternatively you can also refer https://github.com/SAP-samples/abap-platform-rap100/blob/main/exercises/ex04

Task2: Enhance determination with Joule Code Prediction Capability

Use Joule's Predictive Code Completion feature to complete your code by providing simple prompts for each step in the logic.

Here please note that we should use the Toggle option to disable / enable Joule Code Prediction Capability by pressing  button in the toolbar.

Hint: Disable the Joule Code Prediction Capability if you want to give prompts for new steps / once finished to stop Automatic Code Prediction.

Note:- The logic and examples are mentioned just for your reference and to make the purpose of the challenge easy to understand. Please feel free to use your own logic and dataset.

To know how to use Predictive Code Completion follow this tutorial

Please note that the challenge will remain open for another week for anyone who is running late. 

Before we close for this year we would like to thank everyone who participated. We hope all of you enjoyed the challenge. Let us continue to explore how SAP Joule for developers, ABAP AI capabilities can support us with our day-to-day tasks when working with ABAP code, to increase our efficiency and to lower total cost of development.

Happy Jouling!!!

entry.title: BDC-SAC] General Security Assessment Checklist for Customer Infrastructure Teams
entry.content:
Q) System access permissions must be assignable at both individual and group levels.
A) Based on the created accounts, permissions can be assigned for SAC standard menus and features, as well as file sharing access. In addition, users who can access the system can be restricted and managed.

Q) Authentication procedures must be implemented for system access.
A) Authentication is performed using an email/password combination, and a SAML 2.0-based Single Sign-On (SSO) authentication process can be configured.

Q) Group password policies, including complexity requirements and periodic changes, must be enforced.
A) Password policies can be configured through options such as password length, validity period, ending character rules, and the use of special characters.

Q) Detailed reasons for authentication failure must not be disclosed.

A) In case of authentication failure, only a generic failure message is displayed, and no information such as inferable password hints is provided.
 
Q) Upon successful login, the system should provide previous login information (e.g., login time and IP address) for the account.
A) Upon user login, login information is available in the SAC Activities menu.
Additionally, audit logs can be accessed via the provided REST API to review IP addresses, as well as successful and failed login attempts.
 
Q) The account must be locked after repeated failed login attempts.
A) After n failed login attempts, the account is locked for n hours.
(Default: 5 attempts, 1 hour)
 
Q) An account unlock function must be implemented upon the user’s request.
A) When changing the account password or unlocking the account, the account can be unlocked through the login account (email) method.
 
Q) A session timeout feature must be enforced.
A) The session timeout duration can be configured in the SAC admin menu.
Additionally, a session timeout can be set for the authentication validity period after login.
 
Q) Authentication information, transaction data in e-commerce, and personal data must be encrypted both at rest and in transit.
A) SAC uses a certificate-based HTTPS secure communication protocol, and all data is encrypted during transmission when communicating with other systems and applications. If an HTTPS secure connection is not established, data cannot be displayed or transmitted/received in SAC.
 
Q) User system access logs must be recorded and retained for at least one year, including login/logout history, account ID, login/logout time, IP address, and success/failure status.
A) This is available in the SAC Activities menu, with a default log retention limit of 500,000 records. Capacity can be increased or extended upon request or through periodic (e.g., annual) arrangements. In addition, the IP addresses of users accessing the system, along with success/failure status, can be verified via the provided REST API.
 
Q) User menu access logs must be recorded and retained for at least one year, including account ID, timestamp, IP address, accessed menu, and activity details (e.g., view, export, download)
A) This is available in the SAC Activities menu, with a default log limit of 500,000 records. Capacity can be expanded upon request or through periodic (e.g., annual) arrangements. In addition, users’ IP addresses, along with success/failure status, can be verified via the provided REST API.

entry.title: SCIM3 PATCH API returning 403 Forbidden in SAP Analytics Cloud (GET works, Admin access available)
entry.content:
Hi folks,

I am facing an issue while using the SCIM3 API in SAP Analytics Cloud to update team members using PATCH operation.

API Details

Endpoint:

PATCH  /api/v1/scim3/Groups/<TEAM_UUID>

Authentication:
  • Authorization Bearer Token
Headers Used

  • Authorization: Bearer <access_token>

  • x-csrf-token: <csrf_token>

  • x-sap-sac-custom-auth: true

Payload
"schemas": [
            "urn:ietf:params:scim:api:messages:2.0:PatchOp"
        ],
        "Operations": [
            {
                "op":"add",
                "path":"members",
                "value":[
                    {
                        "value":"d0bbac78-776890-9876hjmd",
                        "type":"User",
                        "display":"newuser",
                        "$ref":"/api/v1/scim3/Users/d0bbac78-776890-9876hjmd"
                    }
                ]
            },
            {
                "op": "replace",
            "path": "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group:description",
            "value": "patch test"
            }
        ]
Issue

The PATCH request consistently returns:

{
"status": 403,
"error":"Forbidden"
}

Observations

  • GET operations on /api/v1/scim3/Groups are working successfully.
  • Team UUID is retrieved correctly.
  • Team type is userGroup.
  • I have full admin access (BI_Admin role) in SAP Analytics Cloud.
  • OAuth Client is configured with API access with App Integration.
  • CSRF Token is fetched and passed correctly.
  • when CSRF token is removed, the API returns 401 Unauthorized.
  • PATCH always returns 403 Forbidden.
Questions
  1. Are there additional permissions required specifically for SCIM3 PATCH operations ?
  2. Does the OAuth client require explicit SCIM provisioning(write) permissions?
  3. Are there any tenant-level restrictions for SCIM3 write operations ?
Attachments
  • PATCH request (headers + payload)

 

  • Response (403 Forbidden)

Any guidance would be helpful

Thanks!

entry.title: Re: Failure o cance GRPO
entry.content:
Cancellation of GRPO requires reversing the original accounting entry. Since no journal entry exists, the system cannot generate a reversal. Additionally, if the GR was posted into a blocked stock / special stock category, cancellation may be restricted until the stock is moved back.

To fix this, verify the following:

1) Check PO Line Item
Verify if the PO item is valuated (check account assignment category, material type, valuation class). If it is non-valuated, no FI posting will occur.

2) Check Material Master
Ensure the material is set as valuated stock. If it is flagged as non-valuated, GR will only update logistics, not accounting.

3) Cancellation Options
If stock was updated, you can reverse the GR in MIGO → Cancellation (movement type 102). If system blocks cancellation, first transfer the stock back to unrestricted (if it went into blocked/special stock). If no stock update occurred, you may need to delete the GR document directly in logistics (no FI impact).

entry.title: Re: Clarifications on use of app Create Outgoing Invoices
entry.content:
Hi,

Create Outgoing Invoice App is a FI customer invoice creation which uses DR as default document type.

In general the document type is hidden, unless you click on Edition Options and chose 'Document Type is ready for Input'. And if you chose document type SA, system will not allow you to post because document type SA is by standard used for only GL Posting and subledger items like ASeet, Vendor  and Customer are not activated in the document type. You cannot skip subledger posting using SA document type, because the Creating outgoing invoice app is created for subledger purpose but not for general GL Posting.

entry.title: Production process mapping from Finished good (Variant configurable) to SCRAP product ( Non-VC)
entry.content:
In a particular Steel industry, HR Coils are being produced as final product from HOT Metal using production order confirmation (CO11N) against Dummy Sales order.


Details of HR Coils material is as below:
Material: HRC_F
Material Type: FG (Finished Good)
Batch Managed: YES
Variant Configurable: Yes
Pricing: Moving Average Price (V)

Due to handling or ageing issue, user department wants to scrap some of the HR Coils batches, further, to be used in SMS shop (Steel Melting Shop) as BOM material (HRC_SCRAP).
For this new material HRC_SCRAP has been created as below:
Material: HRC_SCRAP
Material Type: SCR
Batch Managed: NO
Variant Configurable: NO
Pricing: Standard Price (S) @RS 35000/MT

Requirement:
1. Production of HRC_SCRAP (1:1) from HRC_F (Batch Managed) has to happen through production confirmation only, against a new dummy scrap sales order.
2. If MIGO is used, no accounting entry should be generated during transfer of HRC_F from one dummy sales order to scrap dummy sales order.
3. Only during confirmation of HRC_SCRAP, accounting entry of HRC_F, HRC_SCRAP and PRD (Price Difference) should be generated.

Kindly help in correct mapping of production process in SAP.

entry.title: Re: SV-CLM-IMP-PRC Process Hierarchy
entry.content:
Good morning, 

The External Reference data (including the URL) featured in the template file is stored in our backend during the upload. 

However, it is not displayed in the Process Hierarchy UI. 

This data is stored in form of "Prepared Relations". This will allow you to link those Process Hierarchy nodes to other data. For example, when you later upload a configuration library for that Process Pierarchy, the "Prepared Relations" backend data you maintained in the upload will connect the configuration library to the Process Hierarchy Nodes, and you will see that data in the Libraries UI.

Best regards, 

Dolores

entry.title: Agentic AI Gains Momentum as Partners Build Content‑ and Code‑Based Customer‑Driven Solutions
entry.content:
This edition of Hack2Build – Custom AI Solutions brought together partners who are already building agentic AI solutions aligned to real customer needs. Reflecting SAP’s dual‑path approach to agent development, the edition supported both content‑based agents built with Agent Builder in Joule Studio, and code‑based agents developed with the Joule Studio code editor and SAP Cloud SDK for AI on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP).

Hac2Build Custom AI Solutions March 10-17, 2026

 

Across a focused 7‑day hack phase, followed by a 5‑hour Demo Jam on March 17, 13 partner teams delivered functional prototypes demonstrating how agentic AI can drive efficiency, improve decisions, and transform enterprise workflows across procurement, finance, tax, supply chain, utilities, and sales.

Hack2Build: Custom AI Solutions Edition

Throughout the program, partners worked hands‑on with SAP’s agentic AI stack, including:

  • Agent Builder in Joule Studio for rapid design of content‑grounded agents
  • Joule Studio code editor for pro‑code extensibility, tool creation, and MCP integrations
  • SAP Cloud SDK for AI to integrate LLMs and orchestrate models via SAP AI Core
  • Generative AI Hub for model access, prompt lifecycle, and multi‑model orchestration
  • SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine & Knowledge Graph for semantic search and relationship‑aware reasoning
  • SAP Build Process Automation for human approvals, workflow steps, and document handling

This allowed teams to combine reasoning, retrieval, decision logic, and system actions into autonomous workflows built directly on SAP BTP.

Partners selected to participate in the second edition of Hack2Build Custom AI Solutions, March 10-17, 2026

 
After a week of rapid design and iteration, the Demo Jam brought all 13 partner solutions together in a fast‑paced, five‑hour showcase. Each team presented a fully working agent built around a concrete customer scenario, demonstrating how quickly agentic workflows can be applied to real business processes. 

🏆Winners 

With that momentum, the judges selected three solutions that stood out across technical completeness, workflow clarity, and customer relevance.

🥇Capgemini 

Capgemini’s Demand‑to‑Award Agent is an end‑to‑end agentic workflow built on SAP BTP that automates the procurement lifecycle from initial demand intake through supplier award. Incoming requests from email and Excel are processed using using SAP Build Process Automation and SAP Document AI, then enriched with SAP S/4HANA master data. A Demand Management agent classifies requests into Immediate and Projection categories by evaluating delivery timelines, MRP signals, historical demand patterns, and policy rules. Developed using a blend of low‑code agents in Agent Builder in Joule Studio and pro‑code logic, and supported by SAP Build Process Automation and RAG capabilities in SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine. Downstream agents orchestrate supplier shortlisting, RFQ execution, quotation evaluation, awarding, and purchase‑order creation directly in SAP S/4HANA.  

Capgemini 's Demand‑to‑Award Procurement Agent

🥈DataXstream 

DataXstream’s Churn Risk Agent is a retention‑workflow solution on SAP BTP for sales and account teams using the DataXstream's OMS+ SAP front end. The agent evaluates churn indicators, recommends next‑best retention actions, retrieves promotional pricing and material availability, and prepares a draft customer response—all within a single guided flow. Powered by Joule and containerized ML services deployed on SAP BTP, Kyma runtime and OMS+ integration, the solution consolidates fragmented churn signals into prioritized, actionable guidance tailored for customer‑facing users. DataXstream 's Churn Risk Agent

🥉OpenText 

OpenText’s Cognitive P2P Advisor is an orchestrator agent on SAP BTP supporting invoice‑exception resolution to streamline the procure‑to‑pay (P2P) process. It merges structured data from SAP S/4HANA with unstructured documents stored in OpenText Core Archive to provide contextual insights for each exception. Using the SAP HANA Cloud Knowledge Graph to interpret relationships and hierarchical dependencies, the agent blends graph‑based reasoning with semantic document understanding. The result is a set of structured resolution proposals that guide users through root‑cause diagnosis and remediation in P2P workflows. OpenText's Cognitive P2P Advisor

⭐ Finalists

Beyond the winning solutions, the enthusiasm and creativity shown by the remaining finalists reflected the strength of SAP’s partner ecosystem. Across industries such as finance, supply chain, utilities, sales, and tax, partners delivered content‑ and code‑based agents with clear business relevance and strong potential for rapid customer adoption. Their prototypes demonstrated how agentic AI can be applied to diverse operational workflows, proving that partners are already translating customer needs into practical, scalable agent solutions on SAP BTP.

PartnerAgentic AI Solution
AyesaUtilities Switching Agent
CapgeminiDemand‑to‑Award Procurement Agent
DataRobotOptimization Planning Agent
DataXstreamChurn Risk Agent
DeloitteP&L Advisor – Finance
InflexionBid Advisor
KPMGIntelligent Data Harmonization Agent
MouriTechIntelligent Month-End Close Agent
OpenTextCognitive P2P Advisor
RSG Red SinergiaTax Compliance Assistant with SAP AI
Semos CloudAI SDR Pipeline Agent
TCSAP ResolveX
Thomson ReutersTransaction Tax Analysis and Anomaly Detection Agent

These solutions addressed real‑world customer challenges—from procurement and finance to utilities and tax—demonstrating the range and adaptability of SAP’s agentic development model.

What’s Next

All participating partners now move into a four‑week Build Phase, where they will refine and extend their prototypes with support from SAP product engineering, Joule Studio teams, SAP AI experts, and Partner Ecosystem Success. This phase will focus on strengthening grounding strategies, tool calling, integrations, human‑in‑the‑loop flows, and preparing solutions for customer validation and potential go‑to‑market discussions.

Acknowledgments

Thank you to all partner teams, Judges, and SAP contributors across Joule Studio, Business AI Product Engineering and Regional Implementation Group,   BTP and AI Solution Architects, Partner Excellence Center Leads and Partner Ecosystem Success for making this edition of Hack2Build a milestone in advancing partner‑built agentic AI.

entry.title: Extend SAP Document and Reporting Compliance Based Generic Cash Flow Statements
entry.content:
Cash flow statements are essential for every business, revealing the actual cash movements behind your financial numbers. While SAP S/4HANA already delivers country-specific cash flow reports for many regions, organizations in countries not yet covered — or those needing company-specific customizations — require a way to build their own. That is exactly what the Extension Guide for SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) Based Generic Cash Flow Statements addresses.

This guide, available for both SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and On-Premise/Private Cloud, provides a step-by-step walkthrough for extending the SAP-delivered generic cash flow statement to meet your specific country or company requirements. In this blog post, we provide a concise introduction to help you understand the guide's purpose, the key concepts behind it, and what to expect when you follow the extension process.

1. Background: Cash Flow Statements and the Direct Method Challenge

A cash flow statement tracks cash receipts and payments over a specific period, categorized into three activities: operating (core business), investing (long-term assets), and financing (debt, equity, dividends). Accounting standards allow two methods for presenting the operating section.

The indirect method starts with net income and adjusts for non-cash items and working capital changes. It is easier to prepare but does not show actual cash transactions. The direct method reports actual cash receipts and payments — more intuitive and better for forecasting, but significantly harder to implement. The main challenge is that traditional accounting systems record transactions by account, not by cash flow category. To prepare a direct method statement, you must identify cash transactions, classify each into the right category, and match receipts with payments.

This is where Offsetting Account Determination (OAD) becomes essential. OAD automatically pairs each debit posting with exactly one credit posting in your accounting documents, enabling the system to classify cash flows by their true nature. For example, when a supplier invoice is paid, OAD creates paired relationships that let the system trace exactly what each cash outflow represents — turning an otherwise manual process into an automated one.

2. SAP DRC and the Generic Cash Flow Statement

SAP Document and Reporting Compliance (DRC) — formerly known as Advanced Compliance Reporting (ACR) — is SAP's unified framework for statutory reporting. It provides a single platform to define, configure, execute, and submit regulatory reports across countries and regions.

Within DRC, SAP delivers several generic reports that serve as ready-to-use templates: Generic VAT, Generic Cash Flow Statements, Balance Sheet/Income Statement, and Generic Withholding Tax. The Generic Cash Flow Statement supports both the direct and indirect methods and already powers country-specific reports for 17+ countries including China, Argentina, Turkey, South Korea, Poland, Portugal, Indonesia, and more — with the list continuously expanding.

You can leverage the generic cash flow statement in three ways:

  • Use as-is: Simply assign the delivered report definition to your reporting entity and start generating reports
  • Extend with reference: Create a new report definition that inherits from the generic version, then customize parameters, queries, or event handlers to fit your needs
  • Copy and modify: Duplicate the generic definition entirely and adapt it for countries or scenarios requiring deeper customization

The Extension Guide focuses on the second approach — extending with reference — which provides the best balance of reusability and flexibility.

3. How to Extend: A High-Level Walkthrough

The Extension Guide walks you through a five-step process to create your own DRC-based cash flow statement report. Here is a high-level overview of each step:

Step 1: Preparation — Ensure you have the required business roles assigned. You will need an Administrator role (for configuring report definitions and categories), a Developer role (for creating ABAP classes in your development tenant), and an Accountant role (for running and submitting reports).

Step 2: Create ABAP Classes — In your development environment, create a custom event handler class that references the SAP-delivered generic class. This class handles parameter validation for the report's selection screen. The guide provides sample code you can adapt for your country.

Step 3: Define Report Definition and Category — Using the Define Statutory Reports app, create a new report definition by extending the generic version. The system uses an inheritance model — you can keep inherited values, redefine them, or add new ones. Then create a report category that groups your report with its phases, organizational units, and reporting activities.

Step 4: Configure — Set up a reporting entity through the Setting Up Your Statutory Reporting configuration. This includes assigning your report category, defining periodicity, and configuring cash flow statement versions, field sets for grouping, and offsetting account determination versions.

Step 5: Run and Submit — Execute your new report using the Run Statutory Reports app. You can preview the generated cash flow statement, export it to Excel, and update the submission status to mark the reporting period as complete.

The Extension Guide provides detailed screenshots and instructions for every step, making it possible to complete the entire process even if this is your first time working with DRC.

4. What's Next: Resources and Getting Started

Ready to get started? Here are the resources to guide you through the process:

  • Extension Guide for SAP DRC-Based Generic Cash Flow Statements (PDF) — The complete 25-page step-by-step guide with screenshots, sample code, and configuration instructions
  • Step-by-Step Implementation Guide: SAP DRC Cash Flow Statements — A comprehensive blog with 6 video tutorials covering everything from cash flow statement concepts to real-world configuration examples
  • Extensibility and Reuse in Statutory Reporting (SAP Help Portal) — Official documentation on extending and creating DRC reports

We recommend beginning with the video tutorials from the implementation blog to build a solid understanding of the DRC cash flow solution, and then using the Extension Guide for the hands-on extension work. If you have questions or feedback, feel free to leave a comment below or reach out through the SAP Community.

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Lakshmi Triveni and I am excited to start my journey.
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

My name is Sandireddy Lakshmi Triveni, and I’m a final-year Computer Science student at Chalapathi Institute of Engineering and Technology.I am a SAP Certified Associate with hands-on experience in SAP MM configuration, procure-to-pay cycle execution, and integration with FI and SD modules. Alongside my technical journey, I’ve also achieved a 357-day Duolingo streak, which reflects my persistence and commitment to daily learning.

As part of the Hello SAP Community Initiative, I’m excited to share my experience. You can read more about the initiative in Hello SAP Community Initiative , which gives the full background and context.

What is your professional life dream?

My professional dream is to become a skilled SAP consultant who can bridge the gap between business requirements and technical solutions. I want to empower organizations by simplifying complex SAP processes into clear, practical workflows that anyone can understand.

Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

In five years, I see myself leading SAP MM and S/4HANA implementation projects, mentoring juniors, and contributing actively to the SAP Community through blogs, tutorials, and presentations. I also envision myself expanding into AI-driven SAP solutions, combining my certification in Generative AI with enterprise applications.

What is your hobby?

My favorite hobby is reading books. I love immersing myself in different perspectives and stories, which not only relaxes me but also inspires creativity and fresh ideas in my professional work. Reading helps me stay curious and continuously expand my knowledge.

Share a fun fact/story about yourself that people don’t know

A fun fact about me is that I’m very disciplined when it comes to learning. For example, I’ve maintained a 357-day Duolingo streak without missing a single day. It started as a small challenge, but now it’s a symbol of persistence and consistency in my life qualities I also bring into my SAP journey.

What was the most fun project you ever participated in and why?

The most fun project I worked on was configuring the SAP MM pricing procedure. At first, the steps and counters felt confusing, but once I understood how condition types and schema assignments worked, everything clicked into place. I enjoyed troubleshooting errors and documenting each step in a clear, beginner-friendly way. This project gave me confidence and reinforced my passion for simplifying SAP concepts for learners.

 

My SAP TechEd Bangalore Experience

This year I had the opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore 2026, and it was truly inspiring. Being surrounded by professionals, students, and experts from across the SAP ecosystem gave me a sense of belonging to a global community. I attended sessions on SAP S/4HANA innovations, AI integration, and sustainability in enterprise systems, which broadened my perspective on how technology is shaping the future of businesses.

One of the highlights was interacting with mentors and peers who shared practical insights into SAP MM and its integration with other modules. The hands-on workshops helped me connect theory with real-world applications, and I left with renewed motivation to keep learning and contributing.

And now let’s hear from you!

Which 5 things do you absolutely want to achieve in life? Share your thoughts in the comments . I’d love to read them!

entry.title: Re: Down Payment request Not Supported for fixed assets
entry.content: The error you are encountering is expected behavior in SAP Public Cloud. Direct down payment postings to an asset are not supported. In other words, you cannot create a down payment request directly against an Asset Purchase Order (PO). Down payments must be managed through vendor accounts rather than directly tied to assets. The system enforces this restriction to ensure proper capitalization and compliance with accounting standards. Asset acquisition is only recognized once the vendor invoice is posted and settled, not at the down payment stage.

entry.title: SAP Integration Suite - Client Authentication EKU deprecation
entry.content:
Authors: Adam Csallokozi, David Domokos

  • Let’s first determine your environment
  • Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount
  • 1. Create the CF Subaccount
  • 2. Add required data for the new CF Subaccount
  • 3. Enable Cloud Foundry
  • Verify the Destination Service
  • 1. Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys
  • 2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)
  • 3. Generate the OAuth Token for Destination Service
  • 4. Generate the Certificate viaDestination Service REST API
  • 5. Retrieve the Newly Generated Certificate
  • Create and Load the Client Certificate into S/4HANA (STRUST)
  • Import the Client Certificate into SAP BTP
  • Cloud Integration running in NEO environment:
  • Cloud Integration in the Cloud Foundry environment:
  • Conclusion

Driven by an industry-wide change by Certification Authorities (see SAP KBA 3711618 – Client Authentication EKU Deprecation), newly procured publicly trusted SSL/TLS certificates may omit the “Client Authentication” Extended Key Usage (EKU) starting May 2026 as CAs separate server and client roles. For inbound scenarios that use Client Certificate Authentication (two‑way SSL/mTLS) with SAP Integration Suite, Cloud Integration, or API Management, the client certificate must include the Client Authentication EKU; certificates lacking this EKU will not be accepted by the Integration Suite endpoints.

There are multiple ways to implement this requirement. In this article,  we will demonstrate one of the three supported approaches, including the technical steps needed to generate and use the client certificate in your integration scenarios.

Let’s first determine your environment 

Before starting the implementation, first determine which environment applies to your landscape. The steps in this guide differ depending on the environment used for generating and signing the client certificate:

  • If you are working on Cloud Foundry environment please start with Verify the Destination Service chapter

  • If you are working on Neo environment please start with the Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount chapter

 

Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount

ℹ️ Please note that all steps described in this section can be performed free of charge and do not involve any additional costs ℹ️

⚠️ Please be aware to perform the listed actions you need to have a Global Account Admin role or appropriate admin colleague is required⚠️

If you are working on Neo environment, first create a new, Cloud Foundry (CF) Subaccount. This is required to use the Destination service to sign certificate.

1. Create the CF Subaccount

Go to your Global Account and click on 'Create' then choose 'Subaccount'

2. Add required data for the new CF Subaccount

 Please fill the required fields:

  • Display Name: Choose the desired name for your subaccount
  • Region: Choose the desired region for your subaccount

the rest of the fields will be filled automatically, after that click on 'Create'.

3. Enable Cloud Foundry

 

Once the Subaccount has been created, please open it. Click on “Enable Cloud Foundry” button. In the "Enable Cloud Foundry" popup window, input desired value, then press button "Create". Once see "Cloud Foundry Environment" section get populated with Cloud Foundry organization data, the Subaccount creation is successfully completed. 

ℹ️If you need more detailed guide to complete this step you can check our Help Page: Create a Subaccount ℹ️

Verify the Destination Service

In case you are running Cloud Integration / Integration Suite with CF environment, then please go to the CF subaccount where Cloud Integration / Integration Suite is subscribed. In case you are running Cloud Integration with NEO environment, then please go to the newly created CF subaccount as mentioned in chapter 'Create Cloud Foundry Subaccount'.  Then access  menu 'Entitlements' and confirm 'Destination Service' with service plan 'lite' is listed.

 

1. Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys

In BTP Cockpit, go to ‘Instances and Subscriptions’ on the left, then choose the ‘Create’ button in the top right corner

In the pop-up, set Service to Destination Service and Plan to lite. Select the Space where you have administrator permissions, then enter an Instance Name that follows your naming convention. Once the Create button is enabled, click Create.

⚠️When you enable Cloud Foundry (CF) for a new subaccount, only the org is created by default, no spaces are created. As a result, when you create a Destination Service instance, you may see a popup (as shown below) prompting you to create a space first ⚠️

  The instance is now created and visible on the Instances list.

Next, create a service key for the instance. Click the “lite” link to open the details panel on the right, navigate to the Service Keys section, and choose Create.

 

In the pop-up, enter a name for the Service Key, then click Create.

You should now see the newly created service key listed:

Finally, click View Credentials to display the generated credentials; you’ll use them in the next chapter.


 

2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)

⚠️Command line (CMD) should be opened directly in the folder where the .KEY and .CSR files should be saved + OpenSSL needs to be installed on the computer ⚠️

To generate the private key and the CSR signed by a newly generated private key use openSSL. Execute the following command:

openssl req -utf8 -nodes -sha256 -newkey rsa:3072 -keyout genExample.key -out genExample.csr

⚠️All CSR attributes (shown below) will be overwritten during generation, so avoid spending too much time configuring them ⚠️

The command generates genExample.key (private key). You won’t need it for the API steps, but keep it for later use (for example, when creating a .PFX). As a last step of this chapter, the generated .CSR file needs to be Base64‑encoded. This can be done (in case of Windows operating system) via Windows Powershell.

Open Windows PowerShell in the folder containing genExample.csr and run the command:

[Convert]::ToBase64String([IO.File]::ReadAllBytes("genExample.csr"))

as a result the Base64 encoded value of the .CSR file will be displayed in the PowerShell window:

Please copy that value and save it because it will be needed in a later step, you can save it as simple text file in the same folder as the previously created files.

3. Generate the OAuth Token for Destination Service

In this step the OAuth token will be generated that is needed for the certificate creation. To retrieve that OAuth token, either follow the API documentation, or download our predefined REST API collection from here. This collection will be used for the rest if the steps in the 4th and 5th chapters.

Open the “DestinationServiceTokenGeneration” request and populate its variables with the actual values from the credentials you created in the last step of Chapter 1 (Create the Destination Service Instance and Service Keys).

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)url (https://XXXX.authentication.eu12.hana.ondemand.com)
clientID (in the Body section)clientid (sb-clone9ae33f841f774443XXXXXXXXXXXXX)
clientSecret (in the Body section)clientsecret (1c8d712b-b4ed-42ff-bb17-c2c8db2e166b$ROCeUoa1GXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

After you send the request with your actual values, the response body will include the OAuth token:

 

4. Generate the Certificate via Destination Service REST API

From the collection, open the request 'DestinationServiceCertificateGenerator'

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)uri (https://destination-configuration.cfapps.XXXXXXX.ondemand.com)
Name (in payload of the body)Desired name of the created certificate (TestCert)
CN_value (in payload of the body)Common Name, represents the primary, specific domain name (MyDomain)
Base64_Encoded_CSR (in payload of the body)Paste the Base64 encoded value of .CSR that is created as a last step in the chapter '2. Generate the Private Key and a Certificate Signing Request (CSR)'
BearerToken (in Authorization Header)Value of the token that is generated in the previous step (eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJqaWQiOiJoKys5MWZsblpnVXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

In the response of the API call the HTTP 201 - Created should be appear as response code:

 

5. Retrieve the Newly Generated Certificate

From the collection, open the request 'FetchNewCertificate' and a last step retrieve the created certificate chain.

Collection VariableActual Value from Credentials
host (in the URL section)uri (https://destination-configuration.cfapps.XXXXXXX.ondemand.com)
name (in the URL section)In the previous step created name of the certificate (TestCert)
BearerToken (in Authorization Header)Value of the token that is generated in the previous step (eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJqaWQiOiJoKys5MWZsblpnVXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX)

The API should return HTTP 200 OK, and the payload contains the newly created certificate chain, this can be found in the 'Content' field:

Save this value in a text file (base64.txt), because it is needed to Base64-decode the value to get the certificate chain. To perform the Base64 decoding open Windows PowerShell in the folder containing the recently saved file and run the command:

$base64 = Get-Content base64.txt -Raw
[IO.File]::WriteAllBytes("certChain.txt", [Convert]::FromBase64String($base64))

This command will:

  • read the Base64 string from base64.txt
  • Convert it to bytes
  • Write the bytes to certChain.txt in the folder where the PowerShell was opened

Once the command completes, verify that the created file contains content like:

You can then save it as a .PEM file and generate a .PFX for client certificate based authentication, by referring to the blog “Client Certificate Authentication in SAP Cloud Integration: Configuration and Best Practices,” section 1.1/A, “Generating the Client Certificate (.PFX file) – Using OpenSSL.”

ℹ️BTP Connectivity Service on CN data centers signs the keypair using [CDC Cloud Root CA] ℹ️

Create and Load the Client Certificate into S/4HANA (STRUST)

This section explains how to create and load a client certificate in S/4HANA using transaction STRUST. If you’re connecting from S/4HANA, you can generate the CSR directly in STRUST - no OpenSSL needed. After generating the CSR, use the Destination Service API to sign it and obtain the client certificate, then import the signed certificate back into the client PSE in STRUST for client certificate–based authentication.

 

  1. Create an SSL client PSE in S/4HANA; for exact steps refer to 2. Configuration Steps in the Backend 

  2. Right‑click the PSE and choose Create. (Make sure you are in Change mode.)

  3. Generate new Client Certificate and sign the certificate: STRUST -> Edit -> Create certificate request.

  4. In the popup, select Use PSE algorithm as the Signature algorithm, and press Continue.

  5. Save the signing request, which should have the following format:
    -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE REQUEST-----
    <CSR>
    -----END CERTIFICATE REQUEST-----

  6. Encode the CSR in Base64 (the Destination Service API expects a Base64 string without the PEM headers/footers).

  7. In the DestinationServiceCertificateGeneration request, copy the encoded CSR in the body in the parameter "CSR".

  8. After the certificate is signed by the Destination Service API, Base64‑decode it, then in STRUST choose Edit -> Import certificate response. Import the full certificate chain (from the decoded file).

Import the Client Certificate into SAP BTP

Cloud Integration running in NEO environment:

In Neo, inbound client-certificate authentication is configured by uploading the caller’s public certificate and mapping it to a platform user. This is done in the Certificate-to-User Mapping monitor in the Operations view.

  1. Open your Cloud Integration tenant and switch to Operations.
  2. Go to Manage Security > Certificate-to-User Mapping.
  3. Click Add and upload the client’s public certificate file (.cer). Upload only the leaf certificate of the client, not the chain.
  4. Assign a user name to this certificate. Use the technical platform user that should run the iFlow.
  5. Save the mapping.

Cloud Integration in the Cloud Foundry environment:

To make the client certificate available to your Integration flows, create an external-certificate service key:

  1. Go to the SAP BTP cockpit, open your subaccount, and navigate to Instances and Subscriptions
  2. Select your desired 'Process Integration Runtime' with plan 'integration-flow' service instance
  3. Choose Create under Service Keys
  4. Enter a name for the service key
  5. Set Key Type to External Certificate and paste the PEM-encoded client certificate (only the first/leaf certificate, not the chain) into the External Certificate field
  6. Select Create

For more detailed steps, refer to the KBA 3297437  - Client Cert Authentication for Inbound calls to CPI tenants on CF.

Conclusion

The upcoming EKU deprecation underscores the need to proactively adjust certificate management practices in SAP Integration Suite landscapes. By implementing the recommended steps, organizations can maintain secure, compliant client certificate authentication for inbound scenarios. Preparing early helps prevent disruptions and ensures integrations continue to run smoothly as industry standards evolve.

entry.title: Hello SAP Community! My name is Purnima and I'm Passionate About Building My Career in SAP
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community!

I’m Purnima, a B.Tech graduate from Chalapathi Institute of Technology, currently working on an SAP FICO implementation project.

 

Through this experience, I am developing strong knowledge in Financial Accounting (FI) and Controlling (CO), working with areas such as General Ledger, Accounts Payable, Accounts Receivable, and Cost Center Accounting. I am actively involved in configuration, testing, and business process analysis.

I enjoy understanding how SAP supports financial operations, enhances cost control, and enables data-driven decision-making. I am excited to further build my expertise in SAP FICO.

 

Here are the questions I’ve chosen to answer as a part of the "Hello SAP Community" initiative.

What is your professional life dream?

My professional dream is to become a skilled SAP FICO consultant who can help organizations manage their financial processes efficiently. I aim to contribute to business growth by providing accurate financial insights and improving decision-making through SAP solutions.

Where do you see yourself in 5 years?

In the next 5 years, I see myself as an experienced SAP FICO consultant with strong expertise in implementation and support projects. I aim to work on real-time business scenarios, handle complex requirements, and continuously upgrade my skills in SAP and related technologies.

What is your hobby?

My hobbies include learning new technologies, improving my communication skills, and exploring SAP-related concepts. I also enjoy spending time enhancing my knowledge, which helps me grow both personally and professionally.

What is the best lesson your parents taught you?

The best lesson my parents taught me is the importance of hard work, discipline, and consistency. They always encouraged me to stay focused on my goals, be patient during challenges, and never give up. This mindset helps me stay motivated and continuously improve in my career.

Which 5 things do you absolutely want to achieve in life?

  1. To become a successful SAP FICO consultant with strong expertise in financial processes
  2. To continuously learn and upgrade my skills in SAP and new technologies
  3. To achieve financial independence and stability
  4. To contribute to organizational success through my knowledge and work
  5. To maintain a good balance between my personal and professional life.

My SAP TechEd Bangalore Experience

This year, I had the incredible opportunity to attend SAP TechEd Bangalore 2026, and it was truly a rewarding experience. The event brought together developers, architects, and technology enthusiasts from across the ecosystem, creating a vibrant space for learning and collaboration.


From insightful keynote sessions to hands-on workshops, every moment was packed with value. I particularly enjoyed exploring the latest innovations in AI, cloud technologies, and SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). The live demos and real-world use cases helped bridge the gap between theory and practical application.

One of the most valuable aspects was networking—connecting with like-minded professionals, SAP experts, and industry leaders. These conversations opened new perspectives and ideas that I’m excited to take forward in my work.

 

And now let’s hear from you!

I would love to hear your thoughts.

What is your personal life dream?

Feel free to share your answer in the comments and tell us about your goals and dreams.

I am currently pursuing my B.Tech final year in Computer Science and Engineering. Along with my academic studies, I have completed my certification in SAP End-to-End Business Process. What is your professional life dream?

My hobbies include travelling, learning new technologies and improving my technical skills . I enjoy exploring SAP concepts, practicing different business scenarios, and enhancing my problem-solving abilities. I also like reading about emerging technologies and how they impact industries.

What is the best lesson you have learned so far?

One of the most important lessons I have learned is the value of continuous learning and consistency. In the field of technology, staying updated and practicing regularly is key to success. This mindset helps me stay motivated and focused on my goals.

My SAP TechEd Bangalore experience

I also got the chance to connect with people from different organizations, which helped me understand the wide range of opportunities available in the SAP ecosystem. Through these interactions, I learned how companies are adopting new technologies like Artificial Intelligence and Cloud to transform their business processes.

entry.title: A step-by-step end-to-end integration guide for integrating SAP Cloud ALM with Microsoft Teams
entry.content:
SAP Cloud ALM External API Management enables integration with external task management systems (Jira & Azure DevOps), ITSM systems (like ServiceNow), and communication tools & chat systems (Microsoft Teams) via webhooks and API subscriptions.

Recently, we had a requirement in a customer implementation project to notify the project team members via Microsoft Teams and channel workflow tasks whenever new project tasks, user stories, roadmap tasks, requirements and defects are created.

Here is the step-by-step end-to-end integration guide for integrating SAP Cloud ALM with Microsoft Teams.  

  1. MS Teams Activities:

Create a Team “Basis Team” with Channel Name “Cloud ALM” in MS Teams

 

Create incoming webhooks with Microsoft Teams workflows.

 

Copy the webhook link (below, scrambled for restricting external usage)

https://defaultd93f1efe384dYYYabb9605734XXXda.ee.environment.api.powerplatform.com:443/powerautomate/automations/direct/workflows/8d869ca29b7e4b0cae87cb4ffdfac196/triggers/manual/paths/invoke?api-version=1&sp=%2Ftriggers%2Fmanual%2Frun&sv=1.0&sig=kdr4Ge5uttapaBCiGWZYcxlMizZ-d-B-3p1jcYUPAwJQ

 

  1. Cloud ALM subaccount Activities:

You need access to the SAP Cloud ALM subaccount in SAP BTP Cockpit.

Your user requires the “Connectivity and Destination Administrator” role in the SAP Cloud ALM subaccount.

Create a destination in SAP BTP Cockpit.

 

 

 

 

  1. Cloud ALM application Activities:

Click on External API Management

Webhook Management

Add a new webhook

 

Mappings Management – use the built-in for “Task to MS Teams Chat” listed below while adding a subscription

Subscription Management

Add new Subscription

 

  1. Assign the subscription created above to the Cloud ALM projects

In Manage Projects à Assign “CLOUDALM_MSTEAMS_SUBSCRIPTION” under the subscriptions tab for the required Item Type, say “Project Task”, “Roadmap Task”, “User Story”, “Requirement”, “Defect”.

 

  1. Test the workflow

Now, it’s time to create a project task and check the MS team's channel for notifications.

 

 

Once the project task is created, it should show up in the console section of External API Management (as Task created with 200 OK delivery status)

Finally, the workflows task will appear in Teams and Channels for your action.

Conclusion:

Integrating SAP Cloud ALM with Microsoft Teams is a brilliant move for project transparency. It transforms your ALM environment from a static repository into a living, breathing workspace where the team can react to newly created project tasks, user stories, defects or requirements in real-time.

The above setup applies even to SAP Cloud ALM operations monitoring (say, Health Monitoring, Jobs Monitoring, Integration Monitoring, etc.) to send alert notifications to MS Teams chat using the mapping “Event situation to MS Teams chat” and the resource type in the subscription management must be set to “Event Situation”.

entry.title: SAP Community Hobbies Series : [Not Just a Hobby: A Story of Small Classrooms and Big Dreams]
entry.content:
Hi, My name is Abhijna,  I am  an engineering student and I would like to share about my journey of teaching and connecting with young minds as part of the SAP Community Hobbies series.  I am a student with a deep curiosity for connections in all forms. Whether it’s understanding how data travels through networks or building meaningful connections with people, I find myself drawn to both worlds equally. With a strong interest in electronics and networking, I enjoy exploring how systems work, and at the same time, how small human interactions can create lasting impact. And somewhere between these two, I found a journey that’s just getting started.
Coming from a family where teaching has always been more than just a profession, I grew up witnessing the quiet impact it can create. Among all those influences, my grandfather has been my biggest inspiration. Not because of titles or achievements, but because of the way people still remember him, with respect, gratitude, and stories of how he shaped their lives..

About a year or two ago, I visited a  school, one where my grandfather had once taught. I didn’t go there with a plan. It was just a visit. But what I experienced there stayed with me. The respect people had for him, the way they remembered him, and the number of lives he had impacted, it made me pause. It made me think. What if I could become even a small reason for someone to move forward in life?

That thought stayed. And eventually, it turned into action. I went back not as a visitor this time, but as someone who wanted to give. I started small. I spent a week teaching dance, just to connect. And then I began talking to students about things they usually don’t get exposed to, what engineering is, what opportunities exist, what lies beyond what they see every day.

And that’s when I truly understood something. There is a different kind of energy in  school classrooms. The students there don’t just sit and listen , they want to learn. There is curiosity, there is respect, and there is this quiet hunger for knowledge. They are waiting for someone to show them what’s possible.

And being able to stand there, even for a short time, felt like a responsibility , and also a privilege. Of course, it wasn’t always easy.

Standing in front of students who are just a few years younger than you makes you question yourself. Will they understand? Will this make sense to them? Especially when you’re trying to explain concepts like electronics or embedded systems, things that even we find complex. But over time, I realized something simple. If you can relate knowledge to life, anyone can understand it. When I started breaking things down into examples they could see and feel , like explaining sensors through real-life situations , their response changed completely. It wasn’t confusion anymore. It was curiosity. One moment, in particular, stays with me. At the end of a session, a 7th-grade student came up to me and confidently named two sensors we had discussed. Just two. But that moment meant everything. Because it showed me that something had stayed with him. And sometimes, that’s all it takes.

A small understanding. A small spark. A small beginning.

There are many misconceptions around  schools, that it’s difficult to engage, that students may not be interested, or that opportunities to contribute are limited. But my experience has been the opposite. When approached in the right way, schools are open. And the students? They are ready. All they need is someone to show up.

When I think about how this connects to the SAP world, I see a strong parallel. SAP is all about creating impact through structured systems, data, and processes. Similarly, what I do involves identifying gaps, organizing knowledge, and delivering it in a way that creates meaningful outcomes. It’s about enabling growth, improving accessibility, and making information useful ,just like SAP does, but in a human, grassroots way.

This journey has also changed the way I look at learning. To teach even a little, I’ve realized I need to keep learning constantly. It’s not a one-way process. It’s a cycle , where giving and learning go hand in hand. And somewhere along the way, this stopped feeling like just a hobby. It became something more personal. Something that connects who I am, where I come from, and what I want to become. Because at the end of the day, it’s not always about doing something big. Sometimes, it’s just about showing up in a small classroom , and becoming a reason for someone to dream bigger.

entry.title: Troubleshooting Guide: "To Be Hired" Status Not Updating in SAP SuccessFactors Manage Positions
entry.content:
Troubleshooting by Scenario:

1. Issues During Import Processes

Scenario A: Job Information Import Not Updating "To Be Hired" Status

When this occurs:

  • Assign or unassign users via job information import

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Follow-up processes not configured

  • Symptoms: Import completes successfully but "To Be Hired" status remains unchanged
  • Solution: Configure HrisSLEventPublishConfig in Manage Data
    1. Navigate to Manage Data
    2. Create "Centralized Services HRIS Event Publishing Configuration" object
    3. If object not found: Configure Object Definitions → Search for "Centralized Services HRIS Event Publishing Configuration" → Make Corrections → Add label → Save
    4. Grant user permissions under Miscellaneous Permissions

Cause 2: Position Management Settings not enabled

  • Symptoms: Imports work but "To Be Hired" status doesn’t adapt
  • Solution: Enable adaptation setting
    1. Go to Position Management Settings → Import tab
    2. Set "Adapt Position 'To Be Hired' Status After Imports" to YES

Scenario B: Termination Import Not Updating Status

When this occurs:

  • Importing termination records
  • API-based termination processing

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Termination imports don’t trigger "To Be Hired" adaptation
  • Reason: System only adapts when position changes occur, not employment status changes
  • Workaround: Manual position record update required after termination

2. Issues with UI Operations

Scenario C: MSS UI Changes Not Updating Position

When this occurs:

  • Using "Change Job and Compensation Info" in MSS
  • Making position changes through Edit button

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Position previously assigned via Job Information History

  • Symptoms: MSS UI changes don’t trigger "To Be Hired" status updates
  • Solution: This is expected behavior - position won’t adapt if originally assigned through Job History
  • Workaround: Use Add New Employee or direct MSS UI for initial assignments

Cause 2: Missing FTE calculation rules

  • Symptoms: Position assignment completes but "To Be Hired" status doesn’t change
  • Solution: Configure FTE calculation business rules
    1. Create business rule using "Calculate Full-Time Equivalent (FTE)" scenario
    2. Set as onChange rule (on custom standard-hours field) OR Set as onSave rule (on jobInfo element)

Cause 3: Missing mandatory fields in position

  • Symptoms: Position change is successful but "To Be Hired" not updated
  • Solution: Complete required position fields
    1. Navigate to Manage Positions
    2. Find affected position → Make Correction
    3. Fill all mandatory fields

Scenario 😧 Job Information History Changes

When this occurs:

  • Making corrections in Job Information History portlet
  • Adding new records in job history

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Changes in Job Information History don’t update "To Be Hired"
  • Solution: Use proper channels for position updates:
    1. Add New Employee wizard for new hires
    2. MSS UI "Change Job and Compensation Info" or Edit button for existing employees

3. Issues with Onboarding and Recruiting Integration

Scenario E: API-Based Onboarding Not Updating Position

When this occurs:

  • Using APIs for onboarding processes

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: Position Management doesn’t support follow-up processes for Onboarding API changes
  • Manual Solution: Update position records manually after API-based hiring

Scenario F: Crossboarding/Internal Hire Issues

When this occurs:

  • Initiating crossboarding via Recruiting
  • Internal transfers and promotions

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Cause: "Enable Additional Internal Hire Activities" setting disabled
  • Solution:
    1. Go to Admin Center → Onboarding General Settings
    2. Enable "Enable Additional Internal Hire Activities" option

4. Issues with Multiple Incumbents and FTE Management

Scenario G: Multiple Incumbent Positions

When this occurs:

  • Positions allowing multiple incumbents
  • FTE-based position management

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: positionControlled field not editable

  • Symptoms: FTE calculations incorrect despite correct assignments
  • Solution:
    1. Go to Configure Object Definitions → Position
    2. Set positionControlled field to "editable"

Cause 2: FTE setting configuration

  • Symptoms: Position not updating when FTE appears reached
  • Solution: Adjust Position Management Settings
    1. Navigate to Position Management Settings → General
    2. Change "Set or Reset 'To Be Hired' Status if an Incumbent’s 'FTE' is Changed" to "Always" (instead of "Only if Planned FTE Value is Reached")

Cause 3: Effective dating logic with future hires

  • Symptoms: Current FTE reached but status not updating due to future-dated assignments
  • Solution: Ensure employees assigned with appropriate effective dates that reflect current staffing needs

5. Issues with Position Reclassification

Scenario H: Position Reclassification Creating New Positions

When this occurs:

  • Using Event Reasons with "Position Reclassification" follow-up activity
  • Position updates result in new position creation

Root Causes & Solutions:

Cause 1: Search setting disabled

  • Solution:
    1. Go to Position Management Settings
    2. Set "Search for Position in Position Reclassification" to "Yes"

Cause 2: Field matching issues

  • Symptoms: System cannot find matching vacant position
  • Solution: Ensure vacant positions have same field values (Department, Division, Custom Fields) or include them in Job-to-Position sync rules

6. Issues with Future Dating and Transition Periods

Scenario I: Future-Dated Hires with transition period

When this occurs:

  • Processing candidates with future start dates
  • Managing transition periods between incumbents

Root Cause & Solution:

  • Expected Behavior: "To Be Hired" reflects effective date, not process completion date.  It is currently not possible to update the 'to be hired' status to NO, if a person is terminated in the future and the 'successor' gets assigned to the same position BEFORE the other incumbent is leaving.

To retrigger the "To Be Hired" status adaptation, navigate to Admin Alerts → Position Follow-up Processes Alerts and retrigger the process

If it still fails, you can raise a ticket to product support.

Reference:
Enabling To Be Hired Status Adaptation on the Position (Change from Employee Data Import) | SAP Help Portal
Adapt the To Be Hired Status on the Position (Change from UI) | SAP Help Portal

entry.content:
How to extend customer records with credit management data.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
